channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: With His New Museum, Obama Offers a Trip to a Parallel America
item.description: The presidential center that opens this month seems out of step with the zeitgeist of the moment and produces emotional reactions among the discontented of the Trump era.
item.dc:creator: Peter Baker and Jamie Kelter Davis

item.title: How Does LA Count Election Ballots? It Begins With a Dog Sniff.
item.description: Counting ballots in California is a complex and lengthy undertaking. In Los Angeles, the choreographed process includes canines, humans and machines.
item.dc:creator: Orlando Mayorquín
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: A Los Angeles County Sheriff’s Department dog checked incoming ballots for harmful substances at the Ballot Processing Center in the City of Industry, Calif., in November.

item.title: Why Los Angeles Mayor Karen Bass Has to Fight for Her Job
item.description: Ms. Bass, a Democrat, has had a roller-coaster first term. She now faces the first runoff since 2005 for an incumbent mayor in the city.
item.dc:creator: Jill Cowan
item.media:credit: Gabriela Bhaskar/The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Karen Bass, center, campaigning at a community center in Plaza de Valle in Los Angeles, last month.

item.title: Voters in California City Favor Permanent Ban on Data Centers
item.description: Monterey Park, outside Los Angeles, appeared to become the first U.S. city where voters passed such a measure.
item.dc:creator: Billy Witz
item.media:credit: Robert Gauthier/Los Angeles Times, via Getty Images
item.media:description: A mostly vacant business park in Monterey Park, Calif., where plans to build a data center galvanized the community in opposition.

item.title: Massie, 3 Other House Republicans Broke From Trump on Iran War Powers Vote
item.description: Four Republicans from different ideological factions crossed party lines to vote with Democrats in favor of reining in the president’s power to wage war unilaterally.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Louisiana ICE Facility Mistreated Immigrants, Federal Investigators Say
item.description: A report by the Department of Homeland Security’s internal watchdog described officers putting one man in a chokehold and stabbing another with a pen.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz and Nicholas Nehamas
item.media:credit: Gerald Herbert/Associated Press
item.media:description: The Winn Correctional Center, an ICE detention facility in Winnfield, La. Multiple instances of mistreatment were documented at the correctional center last year.

item.title: Republicans Begin to Test the Limits of Trump’s Power by Flexing Their Own
item.description: The president’s unilateral and retributive style of governing is starting to hit a wall in both chambers of Congress.
item.dc:creator: Katie Rogers
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump departing the White House in May. On Wednesday, four House Republicans sided with Democrats to demand Mr. Trump withdraw U.S. forces from the conflict with Iran.

item.title: Obscure Group With Trump Ties Plans to Route Funds to His Allies for Legal Fights
item.description: A nonprofit called the Lawfare Defense Fund has raised $36 million to support those who consider themselves unjust victims of the legal system with grants to pay for litigation.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer and Ken Bensinger
item.media:credit: Allison Robbert for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump recently retreated from a highly divisive plan for a $1.8 billion fund to compensate people who claim they were inappropriately targeted for prosecution, potentially including those who participated in the Jan. 6, 2021, riot.

item.title: In Alabama Ruling, Supreme Court Signals Limited Role for Federal Courts in Redistricting Fights
item.description: In an emergency ruling on Tuesday night, the court’s conservative majority gave a first glimpse into congressional district battles under a weakened Voting Rights Act.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court’s emergency Alabama ruling provided a look into how it may handle congressional district battles going forward.

item.title: Trump Signs Executive Order Removing Job Protections From Federal Workers
item.description: The order is a response to resistance during the first Trump administration, when senior career government officials routinely pushed back against policies that appeared to exceed legal boundaries.
item.dc:creator: Eileen Sullivan
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump at the Oval Office on Wednesday.

item.title: ‘I Love It’: Trump Is Still in Favor of $1.8 Billion Payout Fund
item.description: Todd Blanche, the acting attorney general, said on Tuesday that the administration was “not moving forward with the fund, period,” after the plan drew bipartisan backlash.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump on Wednesday in the Oval Office. In one of his first official acts of his second term, Mr. Trump issued a grant of clemency to the hundreds charged in connection with the Capitol attack.

item.title: House Advances New Sanctions on Russia and Aid to Ukraine
item.description: After G.O.P. leaders blocked additional aid to Ukraine, six Republicans and an independent joined Democrats to force the measure to the floor against the wishes of the speaker.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison
item.media:credit: Finbarr O'Reilly for The New York Times
item.media:description: Damage from Russian strikes in Ukraine’s Donetsk region in February. The House took up legislation that aims to target Russia’s oil and gas sector, its primary source of wartime revenue.

item.title: Park Service Awards No-Bid Contract to Cover Bridge Statues in Gold
item.description: Originally estimated to cost $2.4 million, the government will now spend $5 million to restore the bronzes in time for Independence Day.
item.dc:creator: David A. Fahrenthold
item.media:credit: Jared Soares for The New York Times
item.media:description: Workers built scaffolding around one of the two The Arts of Peace statues near the Lincoln Memorial for a restoration project in Washington.

item.title: Eric Swalwell Won More Than 18,000 Votes Despite Suspending His Campaign
item.description: The former House member withdrew from the California governor’s race amid allegations of sexual assault.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Rich Pedroncelli/Associated Press
item.media:description: Eric Swalwell suspended his campaign for governor, but some Californians still voted for him.

item.title: New Intelligence Chief’s Expertise: Pursuing Trump’s Enemies
item.description: Tulsi Gabbard’s replacement as the director of national intelligence has no known national security experience.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.category: Office of the Director of National Intelligence
item.media:credit: Mark Schiefelbein/Associated Press
item.media:description: Republicans on Capitol Hill have raised doubts about the appointment and Bill Pulte’s qualifications.

item.title: Bessent Calls Clash With Bill Pulte a ‘Locker Room’ Fight
item.description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said he had an argument with the housing finance director last year but now supports his appointment as acting director of national intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times

item.title: G.O.P. Revives Immigration Bill, Weighing Ban on Trump’s Fund
item.description: Several Republicans suggested they would insist on adding a measure to bar the president from creating a fund to pay people who claim to be victims of government persecution.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Demetrius Freeman for The New York Times
item.media:description: A vote on Wednesday set the stage for a lengthy vote-a-thon that could culminate in passage of the immigration enforcement bill on Thursday.

item.title: The Narrow Path to a Democratic Senate Runs Through Very Red States
item.description: Democrats cheer there is a way, even as new worries emerge over whether Graham Platner can flip a Maine seat. Republicans remain confident they will prevail in Texas, Iowa and Alaska.
item.dc:creator: Shane Goldmacher and Reid J. Epstein
item.media:credit: KC McGinnis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Democrats believe Josh Turek, who is running for a Senate seat in Iowa, can be competitive despite being in a red state.

item.title: California Had the Most Expensive Governor’s Race in U.S. History. It’s Still Not Over.
item.description: Nearly $316 million was spent on campaign ads in the primary for California governor. Now the general election spending will begin.
item.dc:creator: Laurel Rosenhall
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Steve Hilton, Xavier Becerra and Tom Steyer were leading the vote count Wednesday in California’s primary election for governor, the most expensive gubernatorial contest in the country’s history.

item.title: As Ebola Outbreak Widens, Trump Has Yet to Outline a Plan
item.description: Despite long-established procedures for bringing Americans home for monitoring and treatment, the Trump administration has not said that it will allow those at risk of Ebola back into the country.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli
item.media:credit: Arlette Bashizi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Health workers wearing protective equipment walk through the isolation ward of a newly established Ebola treatment center in Ituri Province, in the Democratic Republic of Congo.

item.title: In Apparent Reversal, Mullin Says Abrego Garcia Could Be Deported to Costa Rica
item.description: Kilmar Armando Abrego Garcia has long said he would willingly go to Costa Rica. His lawyers sent the new homeland security secretary’s comments to the judge handling his deportation case.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: It remained unclear whether Homeland Security Secretary Markwayne Mullin’s statements reflected a genuine change of direction by his department or whether he was simply unaware of the earlier position taken by other Trump officials.

item.title: How a Prisoner Ended Up Alone in Court in a Death Penalty Case
item.description: The episode at the war court alarmed death penalty lawyers, who argued that the judge had a duty to suspend the proceedings to protect the rights of the defendant.
item.dc:creator: Carol Rosenberg
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Marisa Schwartz Taylor/The New York Times
item.media:description: Guantánamo prisoners charged in the Sept. 11 case have had skilled death penalty defenders, known as learned counsel, at all of their hearings since their arraignment in 2012.

item.title: Six Questions Out of Tuesday’s Big Primaries in California, Iowa and Elsewhere
item.description: Who will run California? Can Democrats win Iowa? Here’s what happened, and what might be next.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk and Katie Glueck
item.media:credit: KC McGinnis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voting on Tuesday in Collins, Iowa.

item.title: Toby Doeden and Larry Rhoden Advance to Runoff for South Dakota Governor
item.description: Toby Doeden, a businessman, and Gov. Larry Rhoden advanced to a runoff. A competitive contest emerged after Kristi Noem stepped down as governor to join President Trump’s cabinet.
item.dc:creator: Mitch Smith
item.media:credit: via Toby Doeden for South Dakota
item.media:description: Toby Doeden, a businessman, is seeking political office for the first time.

item.title: Sam Forstag Wins Democratic House Primary in Montana’s 1st Congressional District
item.description: Sam Forstag’s candidacy will test a liberal theory that left-leaning politicians running in Republican strongholds can do better in general elections than moderates have done historically.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei
item.media:credit: Tailyr Irvine for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sam Forstag will next face the Republican nominee, Aaron Flint, who is backed by President Trump.

item.title: Could Democrats get shut out in a left-leaning California district?
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Representative Kevin Kiley, who switched his registration to independent from Republican after his California district was redrawn to favor Democrats, was leading in the Sixth Congressional District primary as of Wednesday morning.

item.title: Mullin Says ICE Training Going Back to ‘Regular Standards’
item.description: The immigration agency had apparently cut training requirements as a part of its enforcement push over the past year.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Markwayne Mullin, the secretary of the Department of Homeland Security, addressed congressional panels on Tuesday and Wednesday.

item.title: Montana Voters Deliver Mixed Results on Shifting Statehouse Rightward
item.description: Hard-line Republicans targeted moderate colleagues in legislative primaries, but both sides claimed victories in the fight for control of the G.O.P.
item.dc:creator: David W. Chen
item.media:credit: Will Warasila for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voting at Gallatin County Fairgrounds in Bozeman, Mont., on Tuesday.

item.title: ‘Infuriated’ Former Judges Take on Trump
item.description: Critics say it is unseemly for retired judges to trade on the prestige of their former positions.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump at the White House last month.

item.title: Why Democrats Are Hopeful About Making Gains in Iowa
item.description: After voting twice for Barack Obama, Iowa swung to the right. But the state has been hit hard economically and by Trump policies while growing disenchanted with its leaders.
item.dc:creator: Pooja Salhotra
item.media:credit: KC McGinnis for The New York Times
item.media:description: Supporters of Josh Turek, a Paralympian who won the Democratic nomination for U.S. Senate in Iowa, during his election night watch party in Des Moines on Tuesday.

item.title: Trump Suggests Vance and Rubio Should Run Together in 2028 Election
item.description: President Trump said Vice President JD Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio should split a ticket, without mentioning who should be at the top.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan Wolfe
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
item.media:description: Vice President JD Vance and Secretary of State Marco Rubio in October.

item.title: Ruth Shack, Early Champion of Gay Rights in Miami, Dies at 94
item.description: She sponsored one of the nation’s first anti-discrimination ordinances for gays and lesbians and later helped make Miami a global arts center.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: Emily Michot/Miami Herald
item.media:description: Ruth Shack in 2016. As a young political leader in Miami, she sponsored an amendment to add “sexual preference” to the list of characteristics protected against discrimination in housing, education and the workplace.

item.title: House Passes Iran War Powers Resolution in Bipartisan Rebuke to Trump
item.description: A measure to direct an end to U.S. engagement in Iran was adopted with a handful of Republicans in support, sending a signal of opposition to the president’s handling of the war.
item.dc:creator: Robert Jimison and Megan Mineiro
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: The vote on Wednesday was the latest reflection of divisions between Republicans in Congress and President Trump on a range of issues as their interests diverge in the run-up to the midterm congressional elections.

item.title: Spencer Pratt Suggests He Can Be a Mayor for all L.A. Residents, as Vote Counting Continues
item.description: The former reality TV star has tried to broaden his platform, talking about Los Angeles’s homelessness and drugs problem.
item.dc:creator: Conor Dougherty
item.media:credit: Alex Welsh for The New York Times
item.media:description: Spencer Pratt, a candidate for Los Angeles mayor, at Langer’s Deli in the city last month.

item.title: Remaining Uncounted Ballots in California Elections Are Likely to Lean Democratic
item.description: Democratic voters returned their ballots later than Republicans in this year’s primaries, which could cause candidate standings to reshuffle as votes continue to be reported.
item.dc:creator: Luke Vrotsos and Christine Zhang
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: In recent years, more than 80 percent of ballots in California have been cast by mail. They’re sorted and processed in large facilities over a period of weeks.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
item.dc:creator: Jill Cowan and Tim Balk

item.title: FBI Kills Suspect in Hostage Situation at Building With Bank in Bakersfield, California
item.description: The authorities had been negotiating with a man inside a school district office in a building in Bakersfield, Calif., that also houses a Chase Bank branch.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan, Isabella Kwai and Francesca Regalado
item.media:credit: David Dennis/Associated Press
item.media:description: Police vehicles on Tuesday at the Chase Bank where a man barricaded himself in Bakersfield, Calif.

item.title: Knicks Fans Invade San Antonio, Chasing a Finals Rematch 27 Years in the Making
item.description: From Spike Lee to die-hard fans haunted by the 1999 finals, New Yorkers have descended on San Antonio in hopes of witnessing the franchise’s first championship in more than 50 years.
item.dc:creator: Edgar Sandoval and Sarah Maslin Nir
item.media:credit: Jordan Vonderhaar for The New York Times
item.media:description: Eric Tom, left, and his wife, Mary Anne, were among a slew of New York Knicks fans descending on San Antonio International Airport ahead of the 2026 N.B.A. finals.
